metadata.source: Starter rare/endangered section seeded from Michigan rare-plant worklist plus non-legal fungi watchlist

records.habitat: Alvar grasslands, bedrock shores, open calcareous habitats
records.reason: Rare native wild populations in Michigan; not ordinary garden chives.
records.field_marks: Round, hollow onion-scented leaves from bulbs; pink to lilac flowers packed in an erect rounded umbel.
records.look_alikes: Garden chives and other onion-like Allium species. The rare entry is about native wild populations in the right habitat, not ordinary escapees.
records.care_note: Do not share exact spots from natural populations.

records.reason: State-listed rare sedge with northern Michigan significance.
records.field_marks: Loosely tufted sedge with arching stolons that may root at the nodes, very narrow leaves, a terminal male spike, and widely spaced perigynia.
records.look_alikes: Other woodland sedges. This one needs close sedge characters, not a quick glance.
records.care_note: Best treated as a verify-with-keys species.

records.reason: Arctic-boreal plant with limited Michigan distribution.
records.field_marks: Low creeping buttercup of cedar swamps with long-stalked leaves cut into three lobes and small yellow flowers; typically shows 3 sepals rather than 5.
records.look_alikes: Other buttercups and crowfoots in wet ground.
records.care_note: Watch for cool cedar-swamp habitat with sphagnum and seep influence.

records.habitat: Bogs, cold rocky shorelines, northern barrens
records.reason: Boreal species at limited Michigan sites.
records.field_marks: Low evergreen mat-forming shrub with crowded needlelike leaves on reddish-brown stems and black berry-like drupes.
records.look_alikes: Bearberry and other low heath shrubs; check the tiny crowded leaves and black fruit.
records.care_note: Often tied to acidic northern barrens, boggy edges, or rocky northern sites.

records.reason: Rare northern gentian with restricted state distribution.
records.field_marks: Opposite very narrow leaves and blue tubular flowers in terminal clusters and upper leaf axils.
records.look_alikes: Other blue gentians and marsh wildflowers; the narrow opposite leaves are the giveaway.
records.care_note: Usually in wet meadow, fen, bog, springy edge, or stream-margin habitat.

records.reason: Rare in Michigan despite wider northern range.
records.field_marks: Small upright plant with narrow nearly glabrous upper leaves and a long narrow cluster of many whitish flower heads.
records.look_alikes: Other pussytoes/everlastings and small asters with pale heads.
records.care_note: Openings in hardwoods and mixed woods are a stronger clue than the flower color alone.

records.field_marks: Tall branching forb with alternate elliptic leaves, creamy white flowers near the stem tips, and reddish finely bumpy fruits later in season.
records.care_note: Known from very limited northern rocky forest settings in Michigan.

records.reason: Of concern because of limited or vulnerable Michigan populations.
records.field_marks: Rounded opposite toothed leaves and yellow tubular flowers with obvious spotting inside the floral tube.
records.look_alikes: Other monkey-flowers and yellow wetland flowers.
records.care_note: Cold calcium-rich seeps and spring runs matter as much as the flower.

records.field_marks: Tiny yellow-green moonwort often barely above the leaf litter; leaf blade highly reduced or absent and the fertile portion looks embedded in a fleshy stem.
records.look_alikes: Very small moonworts and immature grape-ferns.
records.care_note: Easy to miss entirely; avoid trampling likely habitat.

records.reason: Highly sensitive orchid that should not be publicized precisely.
records.field_marks: One oval basal leaf and a single pink-purple slipper-like flower with a crested pouch and yellow hairs.
records.look_alikes: Other small orchids, but the lone leaf plus showy pouch is distinctive.
records.care_note: Extremely sensitive to disturbance and picking.

records.habitat: Rocky northern openings and shore habitats
records.reason: Northern plant with limited Michigan presence.
records.field_marks: Sticky-stemmed aster with sessile untoothed leaves and deep purple flower heads.
records.look_alikes: Other purple asters; the sticky stem and smooth-edged leaves help.
records.care_note: Most Michigan records are from the Keweenaw/Isle Royale orbit.

records.field_marks: Bluish-green rosette when young; leaves densely white-woolly and only lightly spiny; flower heads very pale on open Great Lakes dunes.
records.look_alikes: Other thistles, especially more aggressively spiny inland species.
records.care_note: Stay off dunes and avoid sharing precise plants on fragile shorelines.

records.habitat: Old hardwood logs and mature forests
records.reason: Old-forest associated fungus with localized northern records; good candidate for a rare fungi watchlist.
records.field_marks: White coral-like branching mass on dead wood, with hanging teeth/spines from the branchlets rather than gills or pores.
records.look_alikes: Other Hericium species and coral fungi; this one grows directly from wood and has dangling teeth.
records.care_note: Older fruitbodies yellow and brown, so the best ID window is when fresh and pale.

records.reason: Often treated as uncommon to rare in parts of North America and worth tracking carefully.
records.field_marks: Fan- to vase-shaped fruitbody with lilac to violet tones when fresh; underside has thick wrinkled ridges rather than true gills.
records.look_alikes: Chanterelle relatives and odd vase fungi. Check for purple tones and blunt wrinkled folds.
records.care_note: Usually associated with conifers in cool northern forest.

records.habitat: Boreal conifer forest and mossy ground
records.reason: Striking northern fungus tied to cool, intact habitat and often considered rare.
records.field_marks: Dark brown to black gelatinous cup or barrel on the forest floor, often appearing like a small cauldron filled with shiny dark jelly.
records.look_alikes: Black cups and odd spring ascomycetes.
records.care_note: Most often reported from spruce-influenced forest floor litter.

records.habitat: Conifer forest with specialized soil/fungal communities
records.reason: Associated with older conifer systems and not something people report every day in the U.P.
records.field_marks: Young fruitbodies are pale and velvety and often exude striking red droplets; underside has teeth instead of gills or pores.
records.look_alikes: Other tooth fungi, especially older brown hydnoid species.
records.care_note: The dramatic red droplets fade; older specimens become much less obvious.
